Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (6) - Variable Scope
Last time we covered functions and variables, so now I want to circle back and fill in something I skipped earlier: variable scope.
First, let's recap variable scope in JavaScript. JS has three ways to declare a variable:
We all know these three each come with their own scoping rules. For example,
), so if you don't declare it inside a function, it'll happily leak into the global environment — what people call global pollution:
language-js shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
// Uncaught ReferenceError: qq is not defined
So what's the downside of global pollution? The most obvious one is variables accidentally clobbering each other:
, on the other hand, are scoped to the block. What's a block? Anything wrapped in
// Uncaught ReferenceError: myName is not defined
So when we read JavaScript code, the declaration keyword tells us which kind of scope a variable belongs to. But what about Python? Let's try rewriting that code in Python and see what happens:
language-py shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
First thing we notice: Python doesn't scope to the block. So maybe it scopes to the function instead? Let's actually verify that:
print('myName:', myName) # NameError: name 'myName' is not defined
Yes — Python scopes variable declarations to the
So from these examples we learn that Python variables behave more like JavaScript's
. One thing to watch out for though: Python has no concept of hoisting.
Which means yeah, you can absolutely pollute things if you're not careful:

myName = 'Ray'

if True:
  myName = 'qq'

print(myName) # qq

Next up is the scope chain. JavaScript has this idea of a scope chain — so what is it? In short: if a variable doesn't exist inside a function, JS goes looking for it in the outer scope, and it keeps climbing up and up and up until it finds it:

So when a function doesn't have the variable or function it needs, it automatically goes looking in the outer scope — and that's the scope chain in JavaScript. If you're not familiar with the scope chain, I wrote about it
Now let's pull this back to Python. Does Python have the same idea? Let's take one of the earlier examples and rewrite it in Python:

value = 1
def fu1():
  print(value) # 1

def fu2():
  value = 2
  fu1()

fu2()
rather than failing to find the variable. So Python's variable lookup is very similar to JavaScript's. Does Python have a scope chain? I'd say it does.
To wrap up, I want to talk about
— two pretty special things. First, let's look at a small Python example: a deeply nested set of functions:
In real-world development our vocabulary tends to be limited — for someone with bad English like me, I only ever reach for the same handful of words.
Back to Python. Like I just said, when we build systems we often write a lot of code, so there's a high chance variable names end up repeating, like this:
As a conclusion the code above looks fine — every function has its own

a = 'Ray'
def fn1():
  a = 'AA'
  print('fn1', a) # AA
  def fn2():
    a = 'BB'
    print('fn2', a) # BB
    def fn3():
      a = 'CC'
      print('fn3', a) # CC
    fn3()
  fn2()
fn1()
print('global', a) # Ray
See it? Because we re-declared a variable called
inside every function, Python assumes we want to create a brand-new variable rather than reassign the global
. So this is where we can use Python's
syntax to tell Python: "Hey! This variable is global! Don't create a local one!":

a = 'Ray'
def fn1():
  a = 'AA'
  print('fn1', a) # AA
  def fn2():
    a = 'BB'
    print('fn2', a) # BB
    def fn3():
      global a
      a = 'CC'
      print('fn3', a) # CC
    fn3()
  fn2()
fn1()
print('global', a) # reassigned by fn3, so it gets overwritten to CC
print('global', a) # reassigned by fn3, so it gets overwritten to CC
And that solves our scope problem. As I mentioned earlier, Python doesn't have a variable declaration keyword, so it needs another way to be told "this isn't a redefinition — go grab the outer one."
Conversely, if what you want to grab is a variable inside an outer function (not global), it's the same deal — except you use

a = 'Ray'
def fn1():
  a = 'AA'
  print('fn1', a) # fn1: AA
  def fn2():
    a = 'BB'
    print('fn2 before replace:', a) # fn2 before replace: BB
    def fn3():
      nonlocal a
      a = 'CC'
      print('fn3', a)
    fn3()
    print('fn2 after replace:', a) # changed to CC
  fn2()
fn1()
print('global', a) # Global Ray
print('fn2 before replace:', a) # fn2 before replace: BB
print('fn2 after replace:', a) # changed to CC
At this point you might wonder why
isn't affected. Think about it with the scope chain in mind: when
first and stops climbing. So if
would keep climbing further up — and we can verify that too:

a = 'Ray'
def fn1():
  a = 'AA'
  print('fn1 before replace:', a) # fn1: AA
  def fn2():
    def fn3():
      nonlocal a
      a = 'CC'
      print('fn3', a) # CC
    fn3()
  fn2()
  print('fn1 after replace:', a) # fn1: CC
fn1()
print('global', a) # Global Ray
print('fn1 before replace:', a) # fn1: AA
print('fn1 after replace:', a) # fn1: CC
Through a real example like this, we get to feel once more that JavaScript's scope chain idea carries over nicely onto Python. Did that clear things up a little for your already half-fried brain?

Last time we covered functions and variables, so now I want to circle back and fill in something I skipped: variable scope. We'll look at how Python's scoping differs from JavaScript's var/let/const, why Python behaves a lot like var, whether Python has a scope chain, and what nonlocal and global are actually for.
